channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Trump’s CISA nominee to exit Coast Guard role, but still in running to lead cyber agency
item.description: Sean Plankey has been serving in a Coast Guard advisory role for some months and is still set to be the nominee to lead CISA.

item.title: Congress to Vote on Ending the War on Iran
item.description: Democrats describe the assault as illegal and contrary to U.S. interests.

item.title: The Trump Administration’s Iran War Justifications Keep Changing
item.description: The U.S. president and his officials can’t get their story straight.

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu is the big winner from the Iran war, for now
item.description: To avoid a row with Donald Trump, Western allies are endorsing Israeli theories of war

item.title: How China Sees the War in Iran
item.description: Beijing is an unentangled superpower—but one with key interests at stake.

item.title: The perils of Donald Trump’s pivot from peace to war president
item.description: If the war fails to pay off politically, will he cut his losses or double down?

item.title: The Army just launched an open call for industry ideas
item.description: A request for information invites potential partners to pitch the service on new ways to jointly fund and execute needed programs.

item.title: Americans Stranded in Middle East as U.S. Urges Its Citizens to Depart
item.description: Most U.S. citizens were not warned to leave the region before the United States and Israel launched the war on Iran.

item.title: Pentagon’s war on Anthropic based on ‘dubious’ legal thinking and ideology—not real risk, sources say
item.description: The company will "likely file suit against everybody,” one legal expert said.

item.title: Insurers Are Keeping Ships Away From the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: As chaos spreads from Iran, global shipping is buckling under the strain.

item.title: Why war isn’t always good for defence stocks
item.description: They win only if governments want just enough weapons—but not too many

item.title: The nightmare scenario energy markets feared is becoming reality
item.description: The longer the war in the Gulf, the harsher the global economic fallout

item.title: The nightmare Iran energy scenario is becoming reality
item.description: A longer war means a harsher global economic fallout

item.title: Are Gulf states running out of missile interceptors?
item.description: Their rulers insist not but they are burning through their stockpiles fast

item.title: The U.S. Campaign in Iran Ignores the Lessons of the Iraq War
Witkoff and Kushner Get an F in Diplomacy
item.description: Inexperienced and overextended, Trump’s envoys have failed on three fronts.

item.title: The Iran war is a jolt to Dubai’s business model
item.description: Can the emirate keep people and investors coming?

item.title: Americans Are Skeptical About Operation Epic Fury
A Guide to Trump’s Second-Term Military Strikes and Actions
Europe Abandons Both Integrity and Influence on Iran
item.description: In response to Trump’s strikes, European leaders have created an alternate reality to escape their hypocrisy.

item.title: The D Brief: Iran war spreads across Mideast; Trump: ‘Wars can be fought forever’; Iranian, US death tolls rise; State urges evacuations from 16 nations; And a bit more.
Magical Thinking Won’t Make Europe an AI Power
item.description: As long as the European Union remains dependent on a handful of US tech companies, its ambitions to become a global leader in AI will remain out of reach. In today’s geopolitical landscape, strategic autonomy is defined by ownership and control of critical infrastructure.

item.title: What the Iran-US War Means for Asia
item.description: As conflict unfurls across the Middle East, Asian countries – U.S. friends and foes alike – are watching carefully.

item.title: It’s Time to Retire the Pottery Barn Rule
item.description: The war on Iran sounds the death knell for the old-school idea of “you break it, you own it.”

item.title: What France’s new nuclear-arms doctrine means for Europe
item.description: Emmanuel Macron will build more warheads and include allies

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The new cancel culture on campuses
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, reports on the pressures facing American universities in the Trump era

item.title: Why the US Strikes on Iran Won’t Derail the Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: The institutional machinery driving the Beijing summit is already too far advanced to reverse, and the Iran crisis has made holding it more urgent, not less.

item.title: A Middle Powers Club Would Make the World More Dangerous
item.description: Mark Carney’s tour of the Indo-Pacific this week is a rousing show of defiance, but it may have unintended effects.

item.title: How Protectionism Sank American Shipping
item.description: More than a century of shielding domestic shipbuilding and shipping has left the industry less competitive than ever. President Donald Trump’s latest effort to revitalize it risks making matters worse by driving up trade costs and entrenching the very structural weaknesses it purports to fix.

item.title: Peru and the Limits of Alarmism About Chinese Investment
item.description: Legal, political, and sovereign constraints make the militarization of the Chinese-operated Port of Chancay far less plausible than critics suggest.

item.title: The Untold Story of Japan’s Election: The Quiet Breakthrough of Team Mirai
item.description: Team Mirai, a new party founded in May 2025, secured 11 seats in the lower house.

item.title: Cuba’s economic divides are widening
item.description: Luck and guile increasingly determine who gets how much

item.title: Sanctions Evasion, Statecraft, and the New Crypto Geography in the Asia-Pacific
item.description: In recent years, enforcement agencies have publicly attributed large-scale cryptocurrency theft and laundering activity to state-aligned actors in the region.

item.title: Reimagining the Economics of Culture
item.description: Standard economic frameworks and metrics for assessing economic output have systematically undervalued the dynamic, long-run returns that culture and the arts generate. Major cultural events like Brazil’s Carnival highlight this failure – and offer a powerful corrective to it.

item.title: The US Attack on Iran Is Reshaping the World Order
item.description: The United States risks a permanent quagmire in the Middle East – which will also redefine the strategic balance of the Korean Peninsula and beyond.

item.title: Why Ali Khamenei may have welcomed the nature of his death
item.description: The ideology of martyrdom could prolong Iran’s regime–and the war

item.title: “Bombs will fall Everywhere”: The American, Israeli and Iranian Weapons Being Deployed in Middle East
item.description:
The United States and Israel launched an attack on Iran on Saturday morning, killing Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei as well as several senior regime figures and striking multiple sites across the country. Iran retaliated by firing at targets across the region, including Israel, Bahrain, Qatar, the UAE and other Gulf states. The conflict is […]

The post “Bombs will fall Everywhere”: The American, Israeli and Iranian Weapons Being Deployed in Middle East appeared first on bellingcat.

item.title: Philippine Tourism: Risk, Resilience, and Returns for Private Capital
item.description: For investors, there is real potential – but also real risk.

item.title: Sri Lanka’s Opposition Is Trying to Securitize Organized Crime. Voters Aren’t Buying It, Yet.
item.description: Many Sri Lankans see recent shootings as violence due to underworld rivalry rather than an existential threat to the state.

item.title: Preparing for AI-Enabled Bioweapons
item.description: The prospect of bioweapons developed by non-state actors has lent new urgency to multilateral pandemic preparation and response efforts. Yet at a time when AI has made such threats exponentially greater, the funding model that sustains critical health-security safeguards is collapsing.

item.title: America’s Latest Middle-East Fiasco
item.description: US President Donald Trump did not decide to go to war against Iran because he had run out of diplomatic options, or because an orderly regime change had become realistic, or because military strikes would neutralize any imminent security threat. He launched another US war in the Middle East – toppling a core campaign promise to his MAGA base – to advance his own personal and political interests. Iranians and people throughout the region will pay a high price, but so could Americans if Trump’s lawlessness abroad advances his authoritarian ambitions at home.

item.title: The Big Picture
Afghan Women, Too
item.description: The Taliban’s war on women continues to plumb new lows with the enactment of a criminal procedure code that fails to criminalize most forms of gender violence. On International Women’s Day, the world should recognize, commend, and support the organizations inside Afghanistan that continue to stand up for gender equality.

item.title: Implications of Prolonged Unrest in Iran for Pakistan
item.description: At a time when Pakistan is grappling with a volatile situation along its Afghan border, instability on the Iran-Pakistan border will add to its problems.

item.title: Prabowo’s Peacemaker Campaign Now Extends to Iran
item.description: Indonesia’s president offers to facilitate negotiations after U.S. and Israeli strikes.

item.title: Taiwan Doesn’t Have to Choose
item.description: Cross-strait peace requires working with both Beijing and Washington.

item.title: How Long Can the Iranian Regime Hold On?
item.description: Change is coming, but It won’t be fast.

item.title: One Man’s War
item.description: How constraints on the U.S. president’s war-making authority eroded—and how to restore them.

item.title: One Man’s War
item.description: How constraints on the U.S. president’s war-making authority eroded—and how to restore them.

item.title: How Long Can the Iranian Regime Hold On?
item.description: Change is coming, but It won’t be fast.

item.title: Punch the Monkey is a Viral Star, but Does his Internet Fame Have a Dark Side?
item.description: Animal rights activists say that viral online content about animals like 'Punch-kun' has the potential to drive the illegal wildlife trade.

item.title: Is Cambodia Pivoting to the US by Joining President Trump’s Board of Peace?
item.description: A binary U.S.-China framing obscures the layered and multi-directional character of Cambodia’s current foreign policy.

item.title: Malaysian Parliament Votes Down Proposed 10-Year Prime Ministerial Term Limit
item.description: The Bill was a part of a package of reforms that Anwar announced in January, in response to growing discontent within the ranks of his unity government.

item.title: The Great Liquidation: Russia’s Great Imperial Retreat
item.description: Its ammunition spent on Ukraine, Russia can no longer act on the world stage. The Moscow-Tehran axis is collapsing in real time.

item.title: Israel's ‘Roaring Lion’: Military Target to Political Decision in Iran
item.description: The military operations against Iran have killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and begun a war that threatens to engulf the region, marking Roaring Lion and Epic Fury as significant violent events.

item.title: Myanmar Junta Grants Amnesties to More Than 7,300 Political Prisoners
item.description: The release is designed to reinforce the legitimacy of the incoming “civilian” administration and communicate signals of reform to foreign governments.

item.title: A Bitter Voice from Inside an Israeli Bomb Shelter
item.description: Longtime national security reporter Yossi Melman hears missiles exploding overhead while watching Israeli TV pundits cheer on Netanyahu and the Iran war.
